summary: No-ship: the diff still permits cross-connector secret delivery, can leak secret values into logs, breaks legacy scrypt vault migration for real passphrases, and has an unguarded OAuth refresh race.

findings.title: Unscoped vault consumers still receive the entire vault
findings.recommendation: Remove the legacy fallback that calls `readVaultSecrets()` for connectors without `secret_scope`, or hard-fail such connectors until they declare scope. As written, any vault consumer missing `secret_scope` can read every secret, which violates the per-connector isolation requirement.

findings.title: Legacy scrypt migration only supports the fixed dev passphrase, stranding real-passphrase vaults
findings.recommendation: Support migration from existing user-chosen scrypt passphrases, or provide an explicit one-time migration path that can decrypt legacy vaults with the user's real passphrase before switching to keychain-only. The current code can only decrypt legacy vaults sealed with `__GARRISON_DEV_UNLOCK__`.

findings.title: Log redaction is chunk-local, so secrets split across stream chunks are written to disk in plaintext
findings.recommendation: Redact across chunk boundaries by buffering overlap between writes or by streaming through a stateful redactor. Redacting each `data` chunk independently does not catch secrets that span two chunks, so `stdout.log`/`stderr.log` can persist plaintext secret values.

findings.title: OAuth refresh is unsynchronized and can lose rotated refresh tokens or fail concurrent callers
findings.recommendation: Serialize refresh per connector or use compare-and-swap style persistence around the grant update. Today, two concurrent `getAccessToken()` calls can refresh the same expired grant in parallel, racing on a rotating refresh token and causing token loss or avoidable failures.
